summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Patient reports positive lab work for hepatitis C and expresses anxiety about the diagnosis.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports being surprised by the positive hepatitis C lab results, as he had not previously been told he had the condition. He states he used to party and even used intravenous drugs but has been clean for over fifteen years. The patient also reports having a beer here and there everyday and smoking one to two cigarettes per day. He mentions having a family history of high blood pressure and diabetes, and he himself reports having depression and diabetes. The patient reports experiencing left hand pain and a grade two out of six systolic ejection murmur was appreciated. He also reports his lungs are clear without wheezes, rales or rhonchi and his abdomen is soft with no hepatosplenomegaly.

Findings
Physical exam revealed a grade two out of six systolic ejection murmur was appreciated on lung auscultation. The patient's lungs were clear to auscultation. The patient's abdomen was soft with no hepatosplenomegaly. Examination findings included a regular rate and rhythm with no distress, and the skin was clear of jaundice. The patient's muscle exam was unremarkable.

Assessment
The patient has hepatitis C and is experiencing anxiety related to the diagnosis. He has a history of substance use and smoking, which increases his risk for liver disease. He also has a family history of hypertension and diabetes.

Plan
The doctor will have the nurse place the orders for a hepatitis C antibody test and a liver panel. Further blood work, including an HCV RNA and genotype test, will be ordered to confirm the diagnosis and assess the severity of liver disease. An ultrasound elastography with biopsy will be ordered to assess fibrosis. The doctor will schedule a follow-up appointment in three weeks to discuss the results and develop a treatment plan. The patient should follow up with his primary care physician for ongoing management of his hypertension and diabetes.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Hepatitis C.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Bruce Ward is a pleasant 60-year-old male who presents to the clinic today following a positive result in a hepatitis C antibody test. He was sent to obtain the hepatitis C antibody test as part of a routine physical. He states he is anxious with the results and denies he has ever been diagnosed with hepatitis C. The patient admits to intravenous drug use in the past; however, he notes it has been longer than 15 years since his last usage. He also reports a history of heavy alcohol use. He continues to drink a beer on occasion. The patient currently smokes 1 to 2 cigarettes per day. He notes he used to smoke more and is having difficulty with complete cessation.

MEDICAL HISTORY

The patient denies any significant past medical history.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient is married with children. He reports history of IV drug use 15 years ago. He currently drinks beer occasionally. The patient reports smoking 1 to 2 cigarettes per day.

FAMILY HISTORY

He reports a family history of high blood pressure, diabetes, and depression.

MEDICATIONS

Patient denies taking any current medications.

VITALS

All vital signs are within normal limits.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CONSTITUTIONAL: In no apparent distress.
CV: Regular rate and rhythm. Grade 2 out of 6 systolic ejection murmur is appreciated.
RESPIRATORY: Lungs are clear without wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
GI/GU: Abdomen is soft with no hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds are present.
SKIN: No jaundice.

RESULTS

The HCV antibody test was reviewed today and is positive.

Liver panel revealed an elevated AST at 39 U/L. The ALT, albumin, and total bilirubin were all within normal limits.

ASSESSMENT

Hepatitis C.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's laboratory findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with him in regard to his current symptoms. His initial labs were consistent with a hepatitis C diagnosis. I have recommended that we confirm the diagnosis with additional blood work including checking his hepatitis C RNA and HCV genotype. I have also recommended that we obtain an ultrasound elastography to evaluate for fibrosis of the liver.

The patient is married with children and is concerned about their hepatitis C status. I advised the patient that his family should be screened and we will assist him with setting appointments with their primary care physician.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 3 weeks to review his results and discuss further treatment.

family history of high blood pressure

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports experiencing left hand pain and a grade two out of six systolic ejection murmur was appreciated. He also reports his lungs are clear without wheezes, rales or rhonchi and his abdomen is soft with no hepatosplenomegaly.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The murmur, lung, and abdominal descriptions are exam findings, not patient-reported symptoms. Placing them in HPI blurs the distinction between subjective history and objective exam.

section_miscategorizations.text: Physical exam revealed a grade two out of six systolic ejection murmur was appreciated on lung auscultation.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The content belongs in Findings, but the phrase 'on lung auscultation' is anatomically inaccurate for a cardiac murmur and could confuse interpretation of the exam location, though it does not change the appropriate section.
